passages.explanation: They discover the island is Malta. The islanders show them unusual kindness — they build a fire and welcome everyone because it's raining and cold. Paul gathers a pile of brushwood and places it on the fire. A viper, driven out by the heat, fastens itself on his hand. The islanders see the snake hanging from his hand and say to each other: this man must be a murderer — even though he escaped the sea, justice won't let him live. Paul shakes the snake off into the fire and suffers no harm. They wait for him to swell up or drop dead, but when nothing happens after a long time, they change their minds and say he must be a god. The crowd's theology swings from 'divine justice' to 'he's a god' in minutes — the same volatility Paul encountered in Lystra.

Near the landing site is an estate belonging to Publius, the chief official of Malta. He welcomes them and entertains them hospitably for three days. Publius' father is sick with fever and dysentery. Paul visits him, prays, places his hands on him, and heals him. When this becomes known, the rest of the sick on the island come and are healed. The islanders honour Paul and his companions in many ways, and when they're ready to sail, they supply everything they need. Paul arrived as a shipwrecked prisoner; he leaves as a healer who's blessed the whole island.

After three months — the winter sailing ban — they set sail on an Alexandrian ship that has wintered at Malta, with the figurehead of the twin gods Castor and Pollux (patron deities of sailors). They stop at Syracuse for three days, then Rhegium, and with a south wind reach Puteoli in two days. There they find believers and are invited to stay a week. Then they head for Rome. Believers from Rome hear Paul is coming and travel out to meet him — some as far as the Forum of Appius (43 miles from Rome) and the Three Taverns (33 miles). When Paul sees them, he thanks God and is encouraged. In Rome, Paul is allowed to live by himself with a soldier to guard him. The journey is complete. Paul reaches Rome — not as a free missionary, but as a prisoner. Yet he has a personal guard, his own quarters, and the freedom to receive visitors.

Three days after arriving, Paul calls together the local Jewish leaders. He explains his situation: he's done nothing against the Jewish people or their ancestral customs. He was handed over to the Romans in Jerusalem, who wanted to release him because he'd done nothing deserving death. But when the Jews objected, he was compelled to appeal to Caesar — not because he has any charge against his own people. He's bound with this chain because of the hope of Israel. The Jewish leaders reply: we haven't received any letters from Judea about you, and no one has reported or said anything bad about you. But we want to hear your views — all we know about this sect is that people everywhere are talking against it. Paul is still following the same pattern: Jews first.

They arrange a day, and a large number come to where Paul is staying. From morning to evening he explains and testifies about the kingdom of God, trying to persuade them about Jesus from both the Law of Moses and the Prophets. Some are convinced; others refuse to believe. They disagree among themselves and begin to leave. Paul makes a final statement, quoting Isaiah 6:9-10 — the passage where God tells Isaiah that the people will hear but never understand, see but never perceive, because their hearts have become calloused. Paul declares: God's salvation has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen. This is Paul's definitive turn to the Gentiles in Rome. The same pattern that played out in Antioch, Corinth, and Ephesus plays out one last time in the capital of the empire.

For two whole years Paul stays in his own rented house and welcomes all who come to see him. He proclaims the kingdom of God and teaches about the Lord Jesus Christ — with all boldness and without hindrance. Acts ends here. There's no trial before Caesar, no verdict, no death. Luke closes with Paul preaching freely in Rome. The word that started in Jerusalem in chapter 1 has reached the ends of the earth — the capital of the known world. The book doesn't end with Paul's story resolved because that's not really the point. The point is the unstoppable advance of the gospel: from a room in Jerusalem to a room in Rome, from Jewish fishermen to the heart of the Gentile world. The word of God is not chained.
